title: Anger in African Healing Traditions
dek: Your anger is not a moral failing. In many African healing traditions, it is recognized as a vital force, a messenger from the body that demands to be heard and integrated, not silenced or shamed.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Ancestral Weight in the Psoas


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T00:13:18.227Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You wake up at 3:00 AM, and the first thing you notice is the tightness in your jaw. The masseter muscle is locked, a physical manifestation of a boundary crossed, a word unspoken. You were taught that this fury is ugly, that it makes you unlovable. But the body knows otherwise. This is not pathology, this is biology. The heat rising in your chest, the way your breath catches in your diaphragm - these are ancient signals. In many African healing traditions, anger is not a sin to be confessed or a flaw to be eradicated. It is a spirit, a force of nature that moves through the fascia and the blood. When you suppress it, it doesn't disappear; it settles into the psoas, the deep muscle of the soul, waiting for acknowledgment. As Resmaa Menakem teaches in his work on somatic abolitionism, trauma and unexpressed anger live in the body. He reminds us that the body is where we live, and it is where the healing must occur. The tension in your shoulders, the levator scapulae pulling tight against the base of your skull - this is the body holding onto what the mind refuses to process. The sweat on your skin at 4:00 AM smells like old copper and fear. You clench your fists, feeling the tendons in your forearms pull taut. This is the body remembering. The ancestors did not view this heat as a curse. They saw it as a fire that could clear the brush, making way for new growth. When you feel the burn in your trapezius, that is the fire asking for air. Gabor Maté often speaks of how the suppression of healthy anger leads to illness. The body turns against itself when it is not allowed to express its truth. The autoimmune response, the chronic pain - these are the body's desperate attempts to speak the anger you have swallowed. Consider the herbs that support this process. We do not use them to numb the pain, but to support the body as it does its work. [Pure Encapsulations Daily Calm](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0DLCX6T4K?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help the nervous system find its footing when the anger feels too large to hold. It is not a cure, but a companion. Your breath is shallow, trapped in the upper chest. The intercostal muscles are rigid. You must invite the breath down, into the belly, into the pelvis. This is where the power lies. The African traditions understand that the pelvis is the seat of life force. When anger is trapped here, it stagnates. We must move it. Bessel van der Kolk reminds us that the body keeps the score. The memory of every time you were told to quiet down, to be a good girl, to swallow your rage - it is all recorded in the tissues. The fascia becomes a map of your silencing. But you can rewrite the map. You can begin by acknowledging the heat. When the anger rises, do not turn away. Feel the way it changes the temperature of your skin. Notice the way your heart rate increases. This is your life force, demanding to be felt. In the tradition of the Sangomas, the traditional healers of Southern Africa, illness is often seen as a calling, a message from the ancestors. Your anger is a calling. It is asking you to step into your power, to set the boundary, to speak the truth. We use [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54ESK?tag=spankyspinola-20) to support the heart when the anger feels like it might break it. Motherwort is the lion-hearted herb, giving us the courage to feel what we have been avoiding. The jaw remembers. The masseter muscle holds the words you did not say. You can massage it, gently, feeling the knots of unexpressed fury. As you do, you might find tears rising. Let them come. The water will cool the fire. Thich Nhat Hanh teaches us to hold our anger like a crying baby. We do not judge the baby; we simply hold it with compassion. But in the somatic reality, holding the anger means feeling it in the muscles, in the bones. It means allowing the trembling in your legs, the shaking in your hands. This is the practice. It is not glamorous. It is sweaty, and it smells like earth and salt. It happens in the middle of the night, when the world is quiet and the body is loud. You might find support in [Herb Pharm Mind Soother (formerly Anxiety Soother)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008CPAKO6?tag=spankyspinola-20), which helps to soothe the frayed edges of the nervous system after a storm of anger has passed. ## A Short Embodied Practice

Find a quiet place where you will not be disturbed. Stand with your feet hip-width apart, feeling the ground beneath you. Notice the tension in your jaw, your shoulders, your belly. Take a deep breath in, and as you exhale, make a sound. It doesn't have to be loud, but it must be true. Let the sound come from your belly, from the deep psoas. Feel the vibration in your chest. Repeat this until you feel a shift in the temperature of your skin, a loosening in the muscles. This is the beginning of integration.

## FAQ

**Q:** Is it safe to feel this much anger?
**A:** It is safer to feel it than to suppress it. The body is built to process emotion. It is the suppression that causes harm.

**Q:** What if my anger hurts someone?
**A:** Feeling your anger and acting on it are two different things. We feel the anger in the body so that we do not have to act it out destructively.

**Q:** How long will this take?
**A:** The body operates on its own timeline. There is no rushing the integration of deep, ancestral anger. Be patient with your tissues.

The body knows the truth of your anger. It holds it in the fascia, in the muscles, in the very marrow of your bones. Listen to it.

---

<section data-author="byline">
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality.
Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T00:13:18.227Z">May 28, 2026</time>.
In our work on anger in african healing traditions, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
</section>
